Failure Severity.description: Discovering failures in undefended models is often insufficient for security evaluation, because modern DNNs are typically deployed with safety, policy, or security safeguards intended to prevent, detect, or mitigate adversarial and safety-violating behavior. A fuzzer may uncover many failures on an undefended model, but such failures do not necessarily reflect weaknesses that an attacker could exploit in practice. Moreover, discovered failures differ in their practical security significance. Failures involving harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating behavior, especially when they bypass explicit safeguards, are more security-relevant than robustness-related errors such as accuracy degradation, mispredictions, or prediction inconsistencies. This metric therefore evaluates the practical severity of the failures that a fuzzer is designed and empirically shown to discover. To assess papers on this metric, we examine the most severe class of failure that is both supported by the fuzzing design and demonstrated in the evaluation, based on the target models, oracle definition, and reported evaluation setting.
Failure Severity.coding_guidance: Use the strongest failure class supported by the fuzzing design and demonstrated in the evaluation. Do not rely only on the paper's motivation, threat discussion, or claimed security relevance.

values.High: Targets a model equipped with explicit safety, policy, or security safeguards, and empirically demonstrates failures that bypass those safeguards to induce prohibited behavior.
values.Medium: Discovers harmful, unsafe, biased,  or toxic, but does not empirically demonstrate bypass of an explicit safeguard.
values.Low: Discovers only robustness-related failures (e.g., mispredictions, accuracy degradation, or prediction inconsistency), without harmful behavior or safeguard bypass.

Targeted Attack Discovery.description: In many realistic threat models, attackers seek to induce specific attacker-chosen outputs (e.g., unsafe content labeled as safe or speech transcribed to chosen commands) rather than arbitrary model errors. For example, an attacker may be more interested in modifying a malicious binary so that it is classified as benign, rather than causing benign software to be classified as malicious. Similarly, an attacker may seek to induce an LLM to provide assistance that satisfies a specific harmful objective, such as generating functional malware code or step-by-step attack instructions. This metric captures whether and how a DNN fuzzer steers its exploration toward predefined attack goals, distinguishing between fuzzers that aim to induce specific attacker-chosen outputs, broader classes of safety- or security-violating behavior (e.g., jailbreaks, toxicity, or unsafe behaviors), and untargeted faults such as misclassifications or prediction inconsistencies.
Targeted Attack Discovery.coding_guidance: Assign the fulfillment level based on the exploration direction and how the oracle defines the attacker objective, not on the the paper's safety or security motivation.

values.High: Supports targeted attack discovery by steering exploration toward attacker-chosen goals with predefined properties (e.g., inducing a classifier to predict a specific target label, speech transcribed to a chosen command, or an LLM to generate malware code with specific functionality).
values.Medium: Steers exploration toward broader classes of safety- or security-violating behavior (e.g., any jailbreak, toxic, biased, or unsafe output), without targeting a specific predefined goal.
values.Low: Performs untargeted exploration where the search objective is prediction deviation or inconsistency, without targeting a specific predefined goal or failure class.

Input Plausibility.coding_guidance: Apply this metric only when the fuzzing design assumes semantically valid or realistic inputs. Assign N/A if the fuzzer intentionally generates unrealistic or semantically invalid inputs to stress-test robustness or explore extreme behaviors.
Input Plausibility.description: Input plausibility is important when a DNN fuzzer assumes that mutations should preserve semantics or remain realistic within the task domain, for two reasons. First, when mutations do not preserve semantics, ground-truth labels become uncertain. Many DNN fuzzers therefore rely on semantic-preserving mutations and oracles that assume semantic equivalence between mutated inputs and their seed inputs. If mutations produce unrealistic or semantically invalid inputs, a changed model output may no longer indicate a meaningful failure, but instead reflect a breakdown of the oracle assumption itself. Second, in realistic threat models, the security relevance of a discovered failure depends on whether it is induced by inputs that remain plausible within the task domain. For instance, a stop sign that is slightly brightened or rotated remains plausible within the driving domain, whereas one corrupted by heavy random noise or visually incoherent textures does not. Similarly, speech containing mild background hiss remains intelligible and natural, while severely distorted or robotic utterances fall outside realistic speech conditions. Failures induced by unrealistic inputs may still reveal robustness weaknesses, but they are less meaningful for practical security evaluation. DNN fuzzers typically generate fault-inducing inputs through iterative mutation of seed inputs. During fuzzing, promising mutated inputs, such as those that trigger failures or expand coverage, are added to the seed pool and selected for further mutation in later iterations. As a result, distortions can accumulate across repeated rounds of mutation. While a single mutation with conservative parameters may preserve semantics, the cumulative effect of repeated mutations can progressively degrade semantic validity, eventually yielding inputs that are no longer realistic or recognizable. Using this metric, we evaluate whether DNN fuzzers enforce input plausibility throughout fuzzing iterations by ensuring that fault-inducing inputs remain semantically valid and within a plausible task-specific input distribution. This metric applies to fuzzers whose design or threat model assumes semantically valid or realistic inputs; fuzzers that intentionally generate unrealistic inputs to stress-test robustness or explore extreme behaviors fall outside its scope.

values.High: Enforces input plausibility throughout fuzzing iterations by explicitly accounting for cumulative distortion across repeated mutations, for example by controlling perturbation magnitude relative to the original seed across iterations or by restricting search to a plausible input distribution (e.g., via a generative model trained to produce realistic inputs).
values.Medium: Applies mutations designed to preserve plausibility at each individual step (e.g., semantic-preserving transformations), but does not explicitly control cumulative distortion across fuzzing iterations (e.g., perturbation magnitude is measured relative to the previous mutated input rather than the original seed).
values.Low: Does not enforce input plausibility, allowing unrealistic or semantically invalid inputs to induce failures.
values.N/A: The metric does not apply because the fuzzer intentionally generates unrealistic or semantically invalid inputs to stress-test robustness or explore extreme behaviors.

Failure Reproducibility.coding_guidance: If the paper does not explicitly describe how input format constraints are handled, infer the fulfillment level from the mutation design and implementation details. Also examine whether the paper discusses or demonstrates failure reproducibility in its evaluation.
Failure Reproducibility.description: Small numerical perturbations introduced during mutation may induce failures that appear only in memory but disappear after saving the mutated input to disk. In DNN fuzzing, mutations are typically applied to in-memory floating-point representations of inputs. If these mutations do not respect the constraints of the target input format, such as image pixel values being integers in the range $[0,255]$, audio waveform amplitudes lying within $[-1,1]$, or text being represented as discrete token IDs, fault-inducing perturbations may be altered or lost when the input is saved to disk, as standard serialization enforces these constraints through rounding and clipping. For example, a pixel mutated from 254 to 256 will be clipped to 255 during serialization, and a pixel changed from 155.0 to 155.4 will be rounded back to 155, in both cases eliminating the perturbation that triggered the failure. This problem is compounded when mutations are applied to normalized representations (e.g., pixel values scaled to $[0,1]$ or standardized using dataset-specific statistics), since perturbations introduced in this space may not survive conversion back to the standard format used for storage. Since real-world DNN systems receive inputs in standard serialized formats (e.g., image files, audio recordings, or text), failures that do not survive serialization cannot be reliably reproduced under realistic deployment conditions and are more likely to be false positives than genuine model weaknesses. Using this metric, we evaluate whether DNN fuzzers account for input format constraints so that serialization does not remove or alter fault-inducing perturbations. Fuzzers that generate inputs directly in serialized formats (e.g., synthesized speech, rendered images, or generated text) are considered reproducible under this metric, since such inputs are inherently stable with respect to serialization.

values.High: Explicitly enforces all input format constraints relevant to the target input representation during mutation (e.g., clipping to valid ranges and rounding to integer precision for images, clipping to valid amplitude ranges for audio), or generates inputs directly in a serialized format (e.g., synthesized speech, rendered images, or generated text).
values.Medium: Applies (1) transformations that are less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss (e.g., rotation or brightness change in images, speed or volume change in audio) without enforcing input format constraints, or (2) fine-grained perturbations while only partially enforcing relevant input format constraints (e.g., clipping but not rounding, or rounding but not clipping).
values.Low: Does not explicitly enforce relevant input format constraints during mutation, allowing fault-inducing perturbations to be altered or lost after serialization.

Failure Diagnostics.coding_guidance: The diagnostic insight must come from analysis of the discovered failures themselves, not from the rationale or theoretical motivation behind the fuzzing design. Methodology motivation, design justification, and hypotheses about potential causes should not be considered as failure diagnostics.
Failure Diagnostics.description: Discovering failure-inducing inputs is often insufficient for security evaluation, because failure cases alone do not reveal what underlying model weakness is being exploited or how it should be addressed. A fuzzing paper that reports many failures but provides no diagnostic analysis leaves developers without guidance on root causes or priorities. This metric captures whether and how a DNN fuzzing paper provides diagnostic understanding of discovered failures. For instance, if failures are concentrated in a particular output class, this may indicate dataset bias that a developer could address through targeted data collection or rebalancing. If failures correlate with specific input patterns or features, this may suggest reliance on spurious or non-robust features that could be mitigated through adversarial training, data augmentation, or regularization. Using this metric, we distinguish between papers that explain why failures occur by linking them to underlying model vulnerabilities, papers that provide partial insight by characterizing properties of the discovered failures, and papers that report failures without any diagnostic analysis.

values.High: Provides diagnostic analysis that explains why failures occur by linking them to underlying model vulnerabilities (e.g., tracing discovered failures to corrupted or biased training data, spurious features, or overfitting)
values.Medium: Provides partial diagnostic insight by characterizing properties of the discovered failures (class-level error concentration, correlations between failures and input structure), or how they correlate with model-internal signals (e.g., neuron activation patterns), without linking failures to underlying model vulnerabilities.
values.Low: Reports discovered failures but does not analyze model internals or failure patterns to provide diagnostic insight.

Attack Transferability.coding_guidance: Examine whether fuzzer discovered fault-inducing inputs are applied to separate target models post-hoc to check whether failures carry over. Multi-model disagreement used as a differential oracle during fuzzing is not cross-model transferability.
Attack Transferability.description: Models trained independently on the same task often exhibit common vulnerabilities, and fault-inducing inputs discovered by fuzzing one model may transfer to others. Such transferability underlies a realistic and widely studied attack setting in which an adversary crafts fault-inducing inputs against a surrogate model and reuses them against an unseen target. Transferable failures are therefore especially important for security evaluation, as they reveal vulnerabilities that extend beyond a single model instance. This metric captures whether and how a DNN fuzzing paper enables the discovery of transferable failures across models. It distinguishes between papers that incorporate explicit design choices to discover transferable failures and demonstrate cross-model transferability, papers that demonstrate transferability by reusing fault-inducing inputs on other models without explicitly designing for it, and papers that evaluate discovered failures only on the model used during fuzzing.

values.High: The fuzzing design incorporates explicit mechanisms to support the discovery of transferable failures across models (e.g., guiding mutation using feedback from surrogate models), and demonstrates cross-model transferability of the discovered failures.
values.Medium: Demonstrates cross-model transferability of the discovered failures, but the fuzzing design does not incorporate explicit mechanisms for transferable attack discovery.
values.Low: Does not demonstrate whether fault-inducing inputs discovered on one model also trigger failures in other models.
